NUTR-1.r: Water is an essential (inorganic) NUTRIENT. It's vital for life but provides no energy (no kilojoules).

NUTR-2.r: White table sugar is a CARBOHYDRATE — specifically the disaccharide sucrose (glucose + fructose).

NUTR-3.r: Marasmus is protein-ENERGY malnutrition — a lack of overall food energy/calories (and protein). (Kwashiorkor is specifically protein deficiency.)

NUTR-4.r: Joint/lubricating mucus is built mainly from CARBOHYDRATES — glycoproteins/mucopolysaccharides (e.g. hyaluronic acid in synovial fluid).

NUTR-5.r: Concentrated sugary cola = a CONCENTRATED ENERGY SOURCE, i.e. refined carbohydrate ('empty calories').

NUTR-6.r: Matching nutrients to roles: carbohydrate → energy; protein → growth & repair; lipid/fat → energy store, insulation, membranes; vitamins & minerals → regulate reactions; water → transport/solvent.

NUTR-7.r: The axon is covered in MYELIN — a fatty (lipid) insulating sheath.

NUTR-8.r: Solidified oils = (saturated / hydrogenated) FATS — e.g. margarine.

NUTR-9.r: Select all — raised troponin indicates CARDIAC MUSCLE DAMAGE, i.e. a heart attack (myocardial infarction) / cardiac injury.

NUTR-10.r: Salivary amylase is an enzyme in saliva that begins CARBOHYDRATE (starch) digestion, breaking starch into maltose. It works best at a neutral pH in the mouth.

NUTR-11.r: Vitamin K — made by bacteria (normal flora) in the large intestine; the liver uses it to make blood-clotting factors.

NUTR-12.r: Correct statement: Vitamin A is needed for vision (especially night vision) — so 'carrots help you see in the dark' has a grain of truth.

NUTR-13.r: Collagen, enzymes and haemoglobin are made from PROTEINS (built from amino acids).

NUTR-14.r: A triglyceride = one GLYCEROL + three FATTY ACIDS.

NUTR-15.r: Semen contains PROSTAGLANDINS (and sex/orgasm releases OXYTOCIN) — both stimulate uterine contractions, which may help start labour.

NUTR-16.r: Coronary plaque (atheroma) is caused by CHOLESTEROL / saturated fat building up in the artery wall (atherosclerosis).

NUTR-17.r: There are 9 essential amino acids that must come from the diet (some older texts list 8 for adults; histidine is the 9th).

NUTR-18.r: A classic biological catalyst is an ENZYME (a protein that speeds up reactions).

NUTR-19.r: Fatty / protein-rich food is best — it slows the absorption of alcohol from the stomach, reducing its toxic effects.

NUTR-20.r: Carbohydrates, lipids and proteins are all ORGANIC (carbon-based) molecules built from smaller building-block units, and all can be used to provide energy.

NUTR-21.r: The baby is likely low in PROTEIN (needed for growth and meeting developmental milestones).

NUTN-1.r: In New Zealand, food energy is measured in KILOJOULES (kJ).

NUTN-2.r: Select all — the major nutrients are: CARBOHYDRATES, PROTEINS, LIPIDS (fats), VITAMINS, MINERALS and WATER.

NUTN-3.r: A balanced diet provides all the nutrients in the right amounts/proportions for the body's needs (with energy in ≈ energy out).

NUTN-4.r: The ~50 molecules the body can't make fast enough and must get from food are the ESSENTIAL NUTRIENTS.

NUTN-5.r: GLUCOSE — a shortage suppresses brain function (the brain's main fuel) and can be fatal.

NUTN-6.r: Glucose is a CARBOHYDRATE — specifically a monosaccharide (simple sugar).

NUTN-7.r: Highly refined carbs (lollies, soft drinks) are called EMPTY CALORIES (concentrated energy, few other nutrients).

NUTN-8.r: PROTEINS help reduce the toxicity of drugs/substances (the liver's detox enzymes are proteins).

NUTN-9.r: Select all — the plasma membrane needs LIPIDS (phospholipids & cholesterol) and PROTEINS (plus some surface carbohydrates).

NUTN-10.r: Animal fats and dairy contain SATURATED FATS (and cholesterol).

NUTN-11.r: Lipids provide/store energy, insulate, cushion organs, build cell membranes, make steroid hormones, and carry fat-soluble vitamins.

NUTN-12.r: Odd-one-out: making antibodies / providing the body's quick immediate energy is NOT a lipid function (those are protein/carbohydrate roles). Lipid roles = energy storage, insulation, membranes, hormones.

NUTN-13.r: A protein-rich diet = meat, fish, eggs, dairy, and legumes/beans.

NUTN-14.r: Incomplete proteins are PLANT proteins missing one or more essential amino acids (e.g. beans, grains, nuts).

NUTN-15.r: Keratin and collagen are made from eating PROTEIN (amino acids).

NUTN-17.r: Amino acids not used to make protein are DEAMINATED in the liver (nitrogen removed → urea) and the remainder used for energy or stored as fat.

NUTN-18.r: Yes — to build a protein, ALL the needed amino acids must be present together, at the same time, in sufficient amounts (all-or-none).

NUTN-19.r: VITAMINS (and minerals) help the body use nutrients — they act as coenzymes/cofactors.

NUTN-20.r: VITAMIN D is the vitamin made by the skin (using UV light).

NUTN-21.r: Vitamin K is needed for BLOOD CLOTTING (the liver uses it to make clotting factors).

NUTN-22.r: Select all — calcium is needed for: strong BONES & TEETH, MUSCLE CONTRACTION, NERVE transmission, and BLOOD CLOTTING.

NUTN-23.r: Iodine is needed to make THYROID HORMONES (thyroxine), which control metabolic rate.

NUTN-24.r: If energy in = energy out, body mass stays the SAME (constant).

NUTN-25.r: Increases in body temperature RAISE the metabolic rate (reactions speed up) — so more energy is used.

NUTN-26.r: TRUE — stretch receptors in the stomach signal the brain via nerves about how much has been eaten (helping regulate intake).

DIGB-1.q: Name the three parts of the small intestine in order from the stomach.
DIGB-1.r: DUODENUM, then JEJUNUM, then ILEUM. The duodenum receives chyme plus bile and pancreatic juice; most absorption happens in the jejunum and ileum.

DIGB-2.q: Which organ makes bile, and where is bile stored and concentrated before use?
DIGB-2.r: The LIVER makes bile continuously; it is stored and concentrated in the GALL BLADDER, which releases it into the duodenum (via the bile duct) to emulsify fats.
DIGB-2.w: Liver makes it, gall bladder stores it.

DIGB-3.q: Trace the upper digestive tract in order from the mouth to the stomach.
DIGB-3.r: Mouth, then pharynx, then oesophagus, then stomach. Swallowed food is pushed down by PERISTALSIS (waves of muscle contraction); the oesophagus itself has no digestive enzymes.
DIGB-3.w: Peristalsis moves food along the whole GI tract.

DIGB-4.q: Name the parts of the large intestine in order.
DIGB-4.r: Caecum (with the appendix), then ascending colon, transverse colon, descending colon, sigmoid colon, rectum, and anus. Its main job is to reabsorb water and form faeces.
DIGB-4.w: Colon goes up, across, down, then S-bend to the rectum.

DIGB-5.q: What are the main functions of the large intestine?
DIGB-5.r: Reabsorb WATER and electrolytes, house gut bacteria (which make some vitamin K and B vitamins), and compact, store and eliminate faeces. Very little chemical digestion happens here.
DIGB-5.w: Main theme: water reclaim + waste storage.

DIGB-6.q: What are the digestive functions of the stomach?
DIGB-6.r: It stores food, churns it (mechanical digestion), and secretes gastric juice — HCl (kills microbes and activates enzymes) plus pepsin, which begins PROTEIN digestion. It turns food into chyme and releases it slowly into the duodenum.
DIGB-6.w: Stomach = protein start + acid + storage; it does NOT absorb much.

DIGB-7.q: Where is the duodenum, and why is it important?
DIGB-7.r: The DUODENUM is the first part of the small intestine, just past the stomach's pyloric sphincter. It receives BILE (from the liver/gall bladder) and PANCREATIC juice, which neutralise stomach acid and digest all three food groups.
DIGB-7.w: The duodenum is where most chemical digestion is completed.

DIGB-8.q: Where does the gall bladder sit, and what does it do?
DIGB-8.r: It sits tucked under the LIVER in the upper-right abdomen. It stores and concentrates BILE, then releases it into the duodenum to emulsify fats — breaking large fat globules into small droplets so lipase can work.
DIGB-8.w: Emulsify = increase fat surface area for digestion.

DIGB-9.q: Where is the rectum, and what is its role?
DIGB-9.r: The RECTUM is the final straight section of the large intestine, just before the anus. It stores faeces; stretching of its wall triggers the urge to defecate.

DIGB-10.q: What guards the anus, and what is the difference between its two sphincters?
DIGB-10.r: The ANUS is the exit of the GI tract, guarded by two sphincters: the INTERNAL anal sphincter (smooth muscle, involuntary) and the EXTERNAL anal sphincter (skeletal muscle, under VOLUNTARY control).
DIGB-10.w: Voluntary external sphincter = why toilet training is possible.

DIGB-11.q: Match each part of the digestive system to its main function: mouth, stomach, small intestine, large intestine, liver, pancreas, gall bladder.
DIGB-11.r: Mouth = ingestion, chewing and starch digestion (salivary amylase). Stomach = protein digestion and storage. Small intestine = digestion completed and nutrients absorbed. Large intestine = water reabsorption. Liver = makes bile. Pancreas = makes digestive enzymes (and bicarbonate). Gall bladder = stores bile.
DIGB-11.w: Liver/pancreas/gall bladder are 'accessory' organs — food never passes through them.

DIGB-12.r: Mechanical digestion (besides the mouth): the STOMACH (churning). Three places of chemical digestion (besides the mouth): the STOMACH, the DUODENUM/small intestine — with enzymes from the pancreas and intestinal wall.

DIGB-13.q: In a villus of the small intestine, what is absorbed into the central lacteal versus into the blood capillaries?
DIGB-13.r: The central LACTEAL (a lymph vessel) absorbs digested FATS (fatty acids). The blood CAPILLARIES absorb GLUCOSE and AMINO ACIDS.
DIGB-13.w: Fats go to lymph first; sugars and amino acids go straight to blood.

DIGF-1.q: Name the structures of the small-intestine wall that increase its surface area for absorption.
DIGF-1.r: CIRCULAR FOLDS (plicae) of the wall, covered in VILLI (finger-like projections), each cell topped with MICROVILLI (the brush border). Every villus contains a LACTEAL (absorbs fat) and blood CAPILLARIES (absorb sugars and amino acids).
DIGF-1.w: Folds x villi x microvilli multiply the surface area enormously.

DIGF-2.q: What are the functions of the villi, microvilli and the lacteal in the small intestine?
DIGF-2.r: Villi and microvilli hugely increase the SURFACE AREA for faster absorption. The villus's blood capillaries absorb glucose and amino acids into the blood; the central LACTEAL absorbs digested fats (fatty acids) into the lymph.
DIGF-2.w: More surface area = more nutrients absorbed per minute.

DIGF-3.q: Match each digestive enzyme to what it digests: salivary amylase, pepsin, pancreatic amylase, trypsin, pancreatic lipase, and maltase/sucrase/lactase.
DIGF-3.r: Salivary amylase -> starch. Pepsin -> protein (in the stomach). Pancreatic amylase -> starch. Trypsin (and chymotrypsin) -> protein. Pancreatic lipase -> fats. Maltase/sucrase/lactase -> disaccharides into simple sugars.
DIGF-3.w: '-ase' enzymes are named after what they break: amyl(starch), lip(fat), prote(protein).

DIGF-4.q: Cholecystokinin (CCK): where is it released, what triggers it, and what does it do?
DIGF-4.r: CCK is released by the DUODENUM when fatty or protein-rich chyme arrives. It makes the GALL BLADDER release bile and the PANCREAS release digestive enzymes, and it slows stomach emptying so digestion can keep up.
DIGF-4.w: CCK = 'chole-cysto-kinin' literally means 'move the gall bladder'.

DIGF-5.q: How can you tell a histology slide is NOT small intestine — what small-intestine features would be missing?
DIGF-5.r: The small intestine has tall VILLI, a MICROVILLI brush border, deep crypts and a central LACTEAL. If those are absent it isn't small intestine — e.g. the stomach has gastric pits and no villi; the large intestine has a flat lining with many goblet cells and no villi.

DIGF-6.q: How do you identify which part of the gut a cross-section is from? Give the tell-tale feature for stomach, small intestine and large intestine.
DIGF-6.r: STOMACH = gastric pits/glands, no villi. SMALL INTESTINE = long villi with a microvilli brush border. LARGE INTESTINE = no villi; a flat lining packed with GOBLET cells (mucus) and straight crypts.
DIGF-6.w: Villi present = small intestine; goblet-rich and flat = large intestine.

DIGF-7.q: What are the finger-like projections of the small-intestine lining called, and what is their purpose?
DIGF-7.r: They are VILLI (each covered in even tinier MICROVILLI, the brush border). They massively increase the surface area to speed up absorption of digested nutrients.
DIGF-7.w: Villi (visible fingers) vs microvilli (microscopic brush on each cell).

DIGF-8.q: What visible features let you identify a slide as small intestine?
DIGF-8.r: The presence of VILLI (finger-like projections) topped by a MICROVILLI brush border, each villus containing a central lacteal and capillaries — a pattern unique to the small intestine.
DIGF-8.w: Villi + brush border together = small intestine.

DIGF-9.r: TRUE — trypsin and pepsin are secreted as INACTIVE zymogens (trypsinogen, pepsinogen) so they don't digest the pancreas/stomach wall; they're activated only in the gut lumen.

DIGSAQ-1.r: Pancreatic juice = digestive enzymes (amylase, lipase, trypsinogen/proteases) + bicarbonate (alkaline) + water. The enzymes are made by the ACINAR (acini) cells; the bicarbonate by the duct cells. It travels down the PANCREATIC DUCT, joining the bile duct at the ampulla of Vater, and empties into the DUODENUM.

DIGSAQ-2.r: Three pancreatic enzymes: (1) Pancreatic AMYLASE → breaks starch into maltose; (2) Pancreatic LIPASE → breaks triglycerides into fatty acids + glycerol; (3) TRYPSIN (from trypsinogen) → breaks proteins/polypeptides into smaller peptides.

DIGSAQ-3.r: Gastric juice — 4 components: (1) HYDROCHLORIC ACID (HCl) — kills microbes, denatures protein, activates pepsin; (2) PEPSINOGEN → PEPSIN — digests protein; (3) MUCUS — protects the stomach lining from acid; (4) INTRINSIC FACTOR — needed to absorb vitamin B12.

DIGSAQ-4.r: Stomach mucosa: gastric pits/glands with PARIETAL cells (HCl, intrinsic factor) and CHIEF cells (pepsinogen); thick protective mucus; folds (rugae). Small-intestine mucosa: VILLI + MICROVILLI (huge absorptive surface), lacteals & capillaries, crypts of Lieberkühn, goblet cells — adapted for ABSORPTION, not acid secretion.

DIGMS-1.r: Ingestion = taking food into the body (through the mouth).

DIGMS-2.r: Sublingual, submandibular and parotid are the three pairs of SALIVARY GLANDS.

DIGMS-3.r: Salivary AMYLASE begins starch (carbohydrate) digestion. (Saliva also moistens food and forms a bolus.)

DIGMS-4.r: The pharynx is the THROAT — the shared passage for food and air behind the mouth and nose.

DIGMS-6.r: The ball of food formed for swallowing is a BOLUS.

DIGMS-7.r: The waves of muscular contraction that move food along the gut are PERISTALSIS.

DIGMS-8.r: The serous membrane covering the stomach is the (visceral) PERITONEUM / serosa.

DIGMS-9.r: The ridges/folds in the stomach are called RUGAE.

DIGMS-10.r: An infant's gastric juice contains RENNIN (chymosin) and gastric lipase to curdle/digest milk, plus HCl and pepsin.

DIGMS-11.r: The stomach enzyme (pepsin) needs a LOW pH because acid (HCl) activates pepsinogen → pepsin and gives pepsin its optimal acidic working conditions.

DIGMS-12.r: After churning with gastric juices, the liquefied food is called CHYME.

DIGMS-13.r: The mechanical + chemical breakdown of food is DIGESTION.

DIGMS-14.r: The two muscular rings (SPHINCTERS) are the CARDIAC (lower oesophageal) sphincter and the PYLORIC sphincter.

DIGMS-15.r: The lower, J-shaped part of the stomach is the PYLORUS (pyloric region).

DIGMS-16.r: The oesophagus is the muscular tube that carries food from the pharynx to the stomach (by peristalsis).

DIGMS-17.r: Four layers of the alimentary canal, innermost → outermost: MUCOSA → SUBMUCOSA → MUSCULARIS (externa) → SEROSA.

DIGMS-18.r: Chemical digestion starts in the MOUTH (salivary amylase on starch).

DIGMS-19.r: A weakened cardiac (lower oesophageal) sphincter → ACID REFLUX / heartburn (GORD) — stomach acid flows back into the oesophagus.

DIGMS-20.r: The stomach expands greatly thanks to its RUGAE (folds) flattening out as it fills.

DIGMS-21.r: With antacids reducing stomach acid, PROTEIN is digested poorly (pepsin needs acid to work).

DIGMS-22.r: HCl is produced by the PARIETAL (oxyntic) cells of the stomach.

DIGMS-23.r: The digestive tract is lined mostly by SIMPLE COLUMNAR epithelium.

DIGMS-24.r: Besides storage/mechanical digestion, the stomach chemically digests PROTEIN (pepsin), secretes intrinsic factor, and kills microbes with acid.

DIGMS-25.r: The nutrients that must be digested are the large macromolecules: CARBOHYDRATES, PROTEINS and FATS. (Vitamins, minerals and water don't need digesting.)

DIGDA-1.r: Accessory organs = salivary glands, teeth, tongue, liver, gall bladder, pancreas. The odd-one-out (part of the tract itself, e.g. the stomach or small intestine) is NOT an accessory organ.

DIGDA-2.r: The HEPATIC PORTAL VEIN carries absorbed nutrients from the gut to the liver.

DIGDA-3.r: The small intestine begins at the DUODENUM.

DIGDA-4.r: No — fats are barely digested before the DUODENUM, where bile emulsifies them and pancreatic lipase digests them.

DIGDA-5.r: The other main function of the small intestine is ABSORPTION of nutrients into blood/lymph.

DIGDA-7.r: Odd-one-out: the liver does NOT produce insulin (that's the pancreas) or mechanically digest food. Its jobs include bile, detox, storage, metabolism and making plasma proteins.

DIGDA-8.r: The haem from old RBCs is turned into BILIRUBIN (a bile pigment); the iron is recycled.

DIGDA-9.r: Odd-one-out: bile contains NO digestive enzymes. Bile emulsifies fats, aids fat & fat-soluble-vitamin absorption, and excretes bilirubin/cholesterol.

DIGDA-10.r: Two true statements: bile is MADE by the liver and STORED/concentrated in the gall bladder; it EMULSIFIES fats (breaks them into droplets) but has no enzymes.

DIGDA-11.r: Pancreatic juice is an alkaline fluid (bicarbonate) + enzymes (amylase, lipase, proteases); it neutralises acidic chyme and digests all food types in the duodenum.

DIGDA-12.r: The structure divided into caecum, appendix, colon, rectum and anus is the LARGE INTESTINE.

DIGDA-13.r: Pancreatic amylase, lipase and trypsin are collectively PANCREATIC JUICE (pancreatic enzymes).

DIGDA-14.r: Large-intestine normal flora make VITAMIN K (and some B vitamins) and ferment undigested fibre.

DIGDA-15.r: The main function of the colon is to REABSORB WATER and electrolytes (forming and storing faeces).

DIGDA-16.r: Trypsin is a pancreatic PROTEASE that digests proteins/polypeptides into smaller peptides (secreted inactive as trypsinogen).

DIGDA-17.r: Segmentation contractions mix CHYME with digestive juices (bile, pancreatic and intestinal secretions) in the small intestine.

DIGDA-18.r: Undigested food is moved along the colon by PERISTALSIS and (mass) HAUSTRAL contractions.

DIGDA-19.r: PARASYMPATHETIC nerves (rest-and-digest) are more active during defecation.

DIGDA-20.r: Most nutrient absorption occurs in the SMALL INTESTINE (via its villi).

DIGDA-21.r: A tummy growl triggered by an ad/smell = the CEPHALIC phase of gastric secretion (sight/smell/thought of food).

DIGDA-22.r: The small intestine is specialised with VILLI and MICROVILLI (and circular folds) — hugely increasing absorptive surface area.

DIGDA-23.r: The colon's greatest contribution is WATER/electrolyte REABSORPTION (and forming faeces).

DIGDA-24.r: Fatty acids (as triglycerides) are absorbed into the LACTEALS (lymphatic vessels) of the villi.

DIGDA-25.r: The small intestine is 'small' because of its narrow DIAMETER (it's actually very long).

DIGDA-26.r: CCK and secretin make the GALL BLADDER release bile and the PANCREAS release enzymes/bicarbonate (and slow stomach emptying).

DIGDA-27.r: Odd-one-out: the liver is least likely to be involved in directly absorbing nutrients across villi, or making insulin — those aren't liver jobs.

DIGDA-28.r: The small blood spaces running between rows of liver cells are the SINUSOIDS.

CARB-1.r: The main purpose of carbohydrate metabolism is to provide ENERGY (ATP) — mainly glucose for cells.

CARB-2.r: The main dietary carbohydrate broken down in digestion is STARCH.

CARB-3.r: Carbohydrates are mainly broken down into GLUCOSE (monosaccharides).

CARB-4.r: Excess glucose is stored as GLYCOGEN (in the liver and muscles).

CARB-5.r: GLUCAGON raises blood glucose between meals (releases glucose from glycogen).

CARB-6.r: Glycolysis = breaking glucose down (into pyruvate) in the cytoplasm, releasing some ATP — the first stage of cellular respiration.

CARB-7.r: It matters in nursing to safely manage blood glucose (diabetes: hypo-/hyperglycaemia), nutrition and energy needs.

CARB-8.r: Carbohydrate metabolism happens mainly in the LIVER and MUSCLE cells (glucose is used by all cells; the liver regulates blood glucose).

CARB-9.r: Amylase breaks down STARCH into maltose/sugars (begins carbohydrate digestion).

CARB-10.r: When glucose is high, insulin promotes glucose UPTAKE into cells and storage as GLYCOGEN (glycogenesis) — lowering blood glucose.

CARB-11.r: Once in a cell, glucose is broken down (glycolysis + respiration) to make ATP, or stored as glycogen/fat.

CARB-12.r: The carbohydrate storage form in the liver and skeletal muscle is GLYCOGEN.

PROT-1.r: Protein metabolism builds and repairs tissues and makes proteins (enzymes, hormones, antibodies); amino acids can also be used for energy.

PROT-2.r: Dietary proteins are broken down into AMINO ACIDS.

PROT-3.r: Protein digestion begins primarily in the STOMACH.

PROT-4.r: PEPSIN starts protein digestion in the stomach.

PROT-5.r: Amino acids are absorbed mainly in the SMALL INTESTINE.

PROT-6.r: Amino acids are used to build new proteins (growth/repair), enzymes, hormones and antibodies — or deaminated for energy.

PROT-7.r: Excess amino acids are DEAMINATED in the liver (nitrogen → urea) and used for energy or stored as fat (they can't be stored as protein).

PROT-8.r: Removing the nitrogen group from an amino acid is DEAMINATION.

PROT-9.r: The liver does deamination, makes UREA, and makes plasma proteins (albumin, clotting factors); also transamination.

PROT-10.r: With inadequate protein intake, the body breaks down its own (muscle) protein for amino acids → muscle wasting; low plasma protein can cause oedema.

LIPID-1.r: Lipid metabolism mainly STORES and PROVIDES energy (fat = the body's main long-term energy store); lipids also build membranes and hormones.

LIPID-2.r: Dietary lipids are mainly digested in the SMALL INTESTINE (duodenum) using bile + pancreatic lipase.

LIPID-3.r: BILE (from the liver) emulsifies fat into small droplets.

LIPID-4.r: LIPASE (pancreatic lipase) breaks triglycerides into fatty acids.

LIPID-5.r: Lipids are broken down into FATTY ACIDS and GLYCEROL.

LIPID-6.r: After absorption, lipids travel in the LYMPH (as chylomicrons via the lacteals), then into the blood, carried by lipoproteins.

LIPID-7.r: Excess lipids are stored in ADIPOSE TISSUE (as triglycerides).

LIPID-8.r: When carbohydrate is low, lipids are broken down for ENERGY (fatty acids → acetyl-CoA → ATP), and can form ketones.

LIPID-9.r: INSULIN promotes fat storage after a meal.

LIPID-10.r: Prolonged fasting/starvation increases fat metabolism, producing KETONES (ketone bodies) — can cause ketosis/ketoacidosis.

METAB-1.r: A synthesis (anabolic) reaction BUILDS larger molecules from smaller ones (e.g. amino acids → protein); it absorbs energy.

METAB-2.r: A decomposition (catabolic) reaction BREAKS a large molecule into smaller ones — e.g. digesting starch → glucose, or glycogen → glucose.

METAB-3.r: Synthesis BUILDS up (small → large, stores energy); decomposition BREAKS down (large → small, releases energy).

METAB-4.r: Glycogen formation (glucose → glycogen, glycogenesis) is a SYNTHESIS (anabolic) reaction.

METAB-5.r: Decomposition reactions are important because they RELEASE energy (and building blocks) — e.g. breaking glucose to make ATP.

METAB-6.r: Anabolism = metabolic processes that BUILD larger molecules from smaller ones (using energy) — growth and repair.

METAB-7.r: Catabolism = breaking larger molecules into smaller ones, RELEASING energy.

METAB-8.r: An anabolic example: protein synthesis (amino acids → protein), or glycogenesis (glucose → glycogen).

METAB-9.r: Anabolism builds up and stores energy; catabolism breaks down and releases energy. Together they make up metabolism.

DNA-1.r: DNA is the molecule that carries the genetic instructions — a double helix of nucleotides; the body's 'blueprint'.

DNA-2.r: The basic structural unit of DNA and RNA is a NUCLEOTIDE.

DNA-3.r: A nucleotide = a SUGAR (deoxyribose/ribose) + a PHOSPHATE group + a NITROGENOUS BASE.

DNA-4.r: URACIL (U) is found in RNA but not DNA (it replaces thymine).

DNA-5.r: DNA carries the instructions to make proteins, and is copied when cells divide, so new cells have the same information for growth and repair.

DNA-6.r: DNA/RNA direct protein synthesis (growth, repair, enzymes, immunity); mutations cause genetic diseases and cancer — all relevant to nursing care.

DNA-7.r: Protein synthesis fill-in: cells make PROTEINS. It begins in the NUCLEUS (DNA holds the instructions). In TRANSCRIPTION a copy is made as mRNA, which leaves for the RIBOSOME in the cytoplasm. There, TRANSLATION assembles AMINO ACIDS in sequence, joined into a POLYPEPTIDE CHAIN that folds into a functional PROTEIN — essential for growth, repair and normal functioning of the CELLS.
